title: E2.7: PearSwarm connection-state stream + honest failure (X8)
description:
CONTEXT: E2 spine, DX X8 (locked D13). On UDP-blocked/carrier-NAT networks P2P can be impossible; today that is an infinite silent wait on swarm.connections. The API must be honest: state stream + bounded timeout + typed failure with WHY.
FILES: packages/flutter_pear/lib/src/swarm.dart, schema.dart/schema.js (state event + failure codes e.g. E_UDP_BLOCKED, E_CONNECT_TIMEOUT), pear-end/index.js (emit state transitions), test/swarm_test.dart (new, against mocks/fake).
STEPS:
1. Define states in schema: discovering, connecting, connected, reconnecting, failed.
2. pear-end emits state events from Hyperswarm lifecycle (join flushed, connection attempts, errors) with failure reason codes where detectable.
3. PearSwarm exposes Stream<PearSwarmState> state; join() gains a bounded discovery/connect timeout after which state=failed with typed reason + docs anchor string.
4. Failure codes registered in the E2.3 err registry so they render via the (later) error catalog.
5. Tests: state sequence on happy path; timeout path reaches failed with typed reason (never hangs); reconnect transition emitted.
VALIDATION: flutter test test/swarm_test.dart green; manual: airplane-mode/blocked network shows failed + reason within the timeout bound, no spinner.

title: E2.5: Session nonce + bundle-version attach handshake (T5)
description:
CONTEXT: E2 spine, eng T5 + codex #6. RPC ids restart at 1 on hot restart (rpc.dart:34) with no session identity, so a stale worklet's late frames can be misattributed to the new Dart session. Native `started` boolean (FlutterPearBarePlugin.kt:~43) cannot carry identity across engine detach. Locked rule: reattach when healthy AND version-matched, else kill+restart.
FILES: rpc.dart, schema.dart/schema.js, pear-end/index.js, FlutterPearBarePlugin.kt, bare_worklet.dart, tests both packages.
STEPS:
1. On worklet start, pear-end generates a session nonce and reports {nonce, bundleVersion} in an attach frame (bundleVersion baked in at pack time by pack.dart).
2. Dart handshake on attach: store nonce; tag no frames (keep wire small) but REJECT inbound frames whose nonce field, when present on attach-scoped events, mismatches; simplest correct form: worklet includes nonce in the attach frame only, Dart drops ALL frames received before a valid attach and re-handshakes on restart.
3. Version check: Dart compares reported bundleVersion to its shipped pear-end version; mismatch -> control-channel kill + fresh start (never converse with a stale bundle).
4. Tests: mock attach with old version -> kill+restart invoked; frames before attach dropped; matched version -> reattach continues session.
VALIDATION: flutter test in flutter_pear and flutter_pear_bare green; on-device: hot restart with unchanged bundle reattaches; with a bumped bundle version restarts cleanly.

BEFORE CLOSING — VALIDATION + REVIEW (mandatory, in order):

2. Run the task-specific tests named in VALIDATION above — all green. New logic MUST have its test in the same diff (no follow-up-test promises).
3. Self-review the diff line-by-line against ACCEPTANCE below; every criterion demonstrably met, none "mostly".
4. Check the parent epic description: confirm no LOCKED decision was reversed or watered down.
5. Every new/changed public symbol has dartdoc.
6. Non-trivial diff (>50 lines or touches rpc/native/pear-end): run /code-review (or a second-model review) and fix P1 findings before close.
7. Post a closing comment: what changed, test evidence (command + result), anything discovered worth a new bead.
acceptance_criteria: Attach frame carries nonce+bundleVersion; EVERY worklet frame carries the session nonce and mismatches are dropped (incl. post-attach arrival, tested); version mismatch triggers kill+restart, match reattaches; all behaviors unit-tested and device-verified across hot restart.
notes: CODEX AUDIT FIX (supersedes step 2's 'tag no frames' simplification — that violated the locked decision): the worklet includes the session nonce field on EVERY worklet->Dart frame (one short field in the envelope, defined in schema); Dart drops any frame whose nonce mismatches the current session. Pre-attach dropping alone is INSUFFICIENT: an in-flight frame from a killed worklet can arrive after the new attach. Add a test: frame with old nonce arriving post-attach is dropped without side effects.

title: E2.6: Native crash observation feeding worklet.onCrash (T1)
description:
CONTEXT: E2 spine, eng T1. LOCKED (codex #5): crash detection is NATIVE — a crashed worklet cannot serialize its own crash envelope, and crashes can predate IPC init. The Kotlin side observes Bare Kit worklet exit/error and reports over the CONTROL channel; Dart exposes worklet.onCrash and fails pending RPC calls.
FILES: FlutterPearBarePlugin.kt, bare_worklet.dart (add onCrash stream + state transition), rpc.dart (subscribe: fail pending on crash), tests both packages.
STEPS:
1. Kotlin: hook Bare Kit's worklet exit/uncaught-error callback (investigate what bare-kit exposes; if only process-level, monitor that); on fire, invoke a control-channel callback into Dart with {reason, detail}.
2. Dart BareWorklet: broadcast Stream<WorkletCrash> onCrash; state -> stopped; crash before first attach also surfaces (the pre-IPC case).
3. PearRpc subscribes: on crash, fail all pending completers with typed E_CRASHED (registry code) carrying the native reason.
4. Debug logging: crash logged loudly in debug mode (CLAUDE.md non-negotiable).
5. Tests: mocked control-channel crash event -> onCrash emits, pending call fails typed, state is stopped; crash-before-attach emits too.
VALIDATION: flutter test both packages green; on-device: kill the worklet (forced JS process exit via a test frame) -> onCrash fires with reason.

title: E1.5: Two-device gate proof — runbook, execution, findings record
description:
CONTEXT: THE GATE. A one-device byte-back only proves module loading + local IPC (codex #3). This task proves the real thing: two independent peers over Hyperswarm — DHT discovery, Noise handshake, mobile networks. GO/NO-GO: if infeasible, the epic and plan STOP for reassessment — record findings either way.
DEPENDS: E1.3 (worklet boots) + E1.4 (swarm relay) on two physical Android devices.
STEPS:
1. Write the runbook: exact build/install/run commands for both devices, expected observations per step, timeout budgets.
2. Execute on two physical devices on DIFFERENT networks if possible (one on wifi, one on mobile data) — same-LAN success alone does not exercise DHT/holepunching honestly.
3. Round-trip one payload A -> swarm -> B -> swarm -> A. Capture latency, connect time, any NAT weirdness.
4. Trigger the forced JS error; confirm typed exception with stack on the Dart side.
5. Record findings in the epic (bd comments): numbers, what broke, design implications. If FAILED: mark epic blocked, write the reassessment note — do NOT quietly continue.
VALIDATION: this task IS the validation. Human executes with agent-prepared runbook; results recorded as a comment on the epic before close.

Same-LAN-only or no-reconnect = the gate has NOT passed.

title: E2: RPC contract spine — reliability, framing, session handshake, connection state
description:
GOAL: Take rpc.dart from a JSON-RPC transport to the locked contract spine that every data-structure package generates from. The schema is the SINGLE SOURCE OF TRUTH (the fake in flutter_pear_test is a conformance consumer, never a co-author — when they disagree, the schema wins and the fake is fixed).

THE FIVE PRIMITIVES (locked — anything beyond these is over-design until usage forces it):
1. request/response (exists: packages/flutter_pear/lib/src/rpc.dart)
2. event-stream subscribe/unsubscribe
3. typed error/crash envelope carrying the JS stack (envelope exists at rpc.dart:68-77; routing does not)
4. binary payload framing
5. chunked-stream + backpressure primitive: RESERVE ONLY (see bulk transport decision below)

WORK ITEMS (all decided in eng review — do not relitigate):
1. T2 per-call timeout: PearRpc.call() (rpc.dart:43) completers currently hang forever on a dead worklet. Add a bounded timeout; on worklet death fail ALL pending completers with a typed timeout error. Test: dead worklet -> Future completes with timeout error, never hangs.
2. T3 typed exception routing: rpc.dart:70 always throws base PearException. Map err.code -> PearConnectionException / PearStorageException (packages/flutter_pear/lib/src/exceptions.dart:24,31). Test: storage error code -> PearStorageException.
3. T4 frame-type discriminator: reserve 1 leading byte on every IPC frame (0x00=JSON control, 0x01=raw binary). Today rpc.dart:57-62 distinguishes frames by "is it valid JSON" and SILENTLY SWALLOWS anything else (line 59) — a future raw binary frame would vanish. Implement 0x00 now; 0x01 handling can be a stub that surfaces loudly (not silently dropped). Both ends: rpc.dart + pear-end/index.js. Test: 0x01 frame routed/flagged, not dropped.
4. T5 session nonce + bundle-version handshake: worklet reports a session nonce AND pear-end bundle version on attach. RPC ids restart at 1 across hot restart (rpc.dart:34) with no session identity — late responses/events from a stale worklet can be misattributed to the new Dart session (codex #6). Dart tags frames with the nonce, rejects stale-nonce frames, kills+restarts on version mismatch. Test: hot restart with changed bundle -> kill+restart not stale reattach; old-session frame ignored.
5. T1 (native half) crash detection is NATIVE, not RPC (locked, codex #5): a crashed worklet cannot serialize its own crash envelope, and crashes can predate IPC init. worklet.onCrash stream is fed by native Bare Kit exit/error observation on the Kotlin side over the control channel (FlutterPearBarePlugin.kt + bare_worklet.dart). RPC timeout (item 1) is the hang-catcher; native observation is the crash-catcher. Test: kill worklet mid-call -> onCrash fires AND pending calls fail.
6. X8 connection-state contract (locked, DX D13): PearSwarm (packages/flutter_pear/lib/src/swarm.dart) exposes a connection-state stream (discovering/connecting/connected/reconnecting/failed) with a bounded connect timeout and typed failure carrying WHY (e.g. E_UDP_BLOCKED) + escape-path doc anchor. On hostile networks (carrier NAT, UDP-blocked corporate wifi) the API must fail honestly — never an infinite spinner. State events defined in the schema; emitted by pear-end.
7. Method-name string literals ('swarm.join', 'connection.write', event names in swarm.dart:58,67-75) become shared typed constants generated/defined by the schema.

BULK TRANSPORT (locked, codex #4 — do NOT build streaming machinery here): benchmark platform-channel throughput first (project_plan.md section 7 committed to this). For Hyperdrive bulk, default to file-path import/export (worklet writes to disk, Dart reads the file) or pull-based bounded reads; FFI stays the escape hatch. Only the frame-type BYTE is reserved now.

CODEGEN (locked): NO codegen yet. Hand-write the first two wrappers against the schema; introduce codegen only if the third wrapper's boilerplate is provably mechanical.

TESTS: every failure path above ships with the code, not as follow-up. Coverage diagram in the plan lists 7 spine gaps: timeout, decode failure, onCrash, typed mapping, discriminator, stale nonce, write-after-close.

Reference: ~/.gstack/projects/andrewloable-flutter_pear/andrewloable-main-design-20260703-131146.md (Eng Review Locked Decisions, Implementation Tasks T1-T5, X8)
acceptance_criteria: All 7 spine failure-path tests green (timeout, decode failure, onCrash native, err.code->subtype, 0x01 discriminator not swallowed, stale-nonce rejected, write-after-close); X8 state stream reaches 'failed' with typed reason within bounded timeout on a blocked network; schema doc is the single source of truth.

title: E1: M1 GATE — real Bare Kit worklet + two-device Hyperswarm round trip
description:
GOAL: Replace the Android native echo with a real Bare Kit worklet and prove a genuine Hyperswarm round trip between TWO independent peers on physical hardware. This is the go/no-go GATE for the whole project: if this fails, the single-release plan is void and the approach gets reassessed before any schema or wrapper work continues.

WHY TWO DEVICES (locked decision, codex #3): a one-device "byte back" test only proves module loading + local IPC. The real gate requires DHT discovery, Noise handshake, mobile networking, and reconnect between two independent peers. The one-evening single-device spike is the FIRST smoke test, not the gate.

CONTEXT / CURRENT STATE:
- Native side echoes: packages/flutter_pear_bare/android/src/main/kotlin/tech/loable/flutter_pear_bare/FlutterPearBarePlugin.kt (see TODO(M1) in startWorklet(), line ~46)
- Dart IPC + lifecycle already work against the echo: packages/flutter_pear_bare/lib/src/bare_worklet.dart
- pear-end JS stub: packages/flutter_pear/pear-end/index.js
- bare-pack --target host --linked flags are UNRESOLVED in packages/flutter_pear/bin/pack.dart (~line 19) — resolving how the bundle is built for Android is part of this gate
- Mirror holepunchto/react-native-bare-kit for the Worklet boot pattern (worklet.start('/app.js', source) + IPC pipe)

WORK ITEMS:
1. Minimal Bare Kit dependency wiring in packages/flutter_pear_bare/android/build.gradle — just enough to boot a worklet (full fetcher/checksum/ABI productionization is the Native Distribution epic, NOT this one)
2. Boot a worklet from the bundled pear-end in startWorklet(); pipe worklet IPC to the existing BasicMessageChannel
3. pear-end: join a hardcoded topic via Hyperswarm, echo received swarm bytes back over IPC
4. Add INTERNET permission if needed for the spike (codex #8 — permanent fix lives in Native Distribution epic)
5. Two-device proof: two physical Android phones discover each other on the topic (DHT), complete Noise handshake, and round-trip one payload phone A -> swarm -> phone B -> swarm -> phone A
6. Force one JS exception in the worklet and confirm it crosses RPC into a Dart PearException with the JS stack attached
7. Write down what broke: latency/throughput numbers, NAT behavior observed, anything that should bend downstream design

CONTINGENCY (locked): if the round trip proves infeasible on real devices, STOP — do not proceed to schema or wrappers. Reassess the whole approach.

Reference: full plan at ~/.gstack/projects/andrewloable-flutter_pear/andrewloable-main-design-20260703-131146.md (sections: Next Steps step 0, Eng Review Locked Decisions, The Assignment)
acceptance_criteria: Two physical devices discover via DHT, Noise-handshake, and round-trip a payload over Hyperswarm through a real worklet booted from the bundled pear-end; a forced JS exception crosses RPC as a typed Dart exception with JS stack. If infeasible: STOP, reassess plan.

title: E6.3: Hot-restart reattach-or-kill via attach handshake
description:
CONTEXT: E6. Hot-restart determinism end-to-end, using the E2.5 handshake (nonce + bundleVersion). LOCKED rule: reattach when healthy AND version-matched, else kill + cleanly restart. FILES: bare_worklet.dart (start() reattach path), FlutterPearBarePlugin.kt (health probe: worklet alive?), tests.
STEPS:

3. Ensure Dart-side singletons/streams from the PREVIOUS isolate generation can't leak (hot restart resets Dart; native must tolerate a re-registered handler).

description:
GOAL: Reproducible builds, enforced licensing, and the pub.dev release train. Publish v0.1 (ANDROID-ONLY — locked; iOS is v0.2 with its own podspec/Xcode/background milestone) once the full stack + lifecycle + license assets are wired. Single release, no preview slices (user's settled office-hours decision — do not relitigate).

WORK ITEMS:
1. T9 reproducible bundle (codex #11): pin pear-end JS deps (drop caret ranges in packages/flutter_pear/pear-end/package.json); STOP .gitignoring lockfiles — commit package-lock.json so the shipped bundle is reproducible.
2. T10 license collector enforcement (codex #12): packages/flutter_pear/bin/pack.dart currently SILENTLY SKIPS modules without license files. Make it: FAIL (not skip) on missing license; reject forbidden SPDX values (GPL/AGPL/LGPL/MPL/SSPL/unlicensed); walk nested dependency trees; include native Bare Kit notices; regenerate NOTICE without stale output. Extend packages/flutter_pear/test/pack_test.dart for each behavior.
3. T11 publishing mechanics (codex #10): flutter_pear has a PATH dependency on flutter_pear_bare (packages/flutter_pear/pubspec.yaml:14) — pub.dev requires separately published packages with hosted version constraints. Define publication order (flutter_pear_bare -> flutter_pear_test -> flutter_pear) and convert path deps at publish time. Publish-on-tag workflow.
4. Compatibility table as CI contract (codex #13 + eng): plugin <-> Bare Kit <-> Hyper* versions PLUS the tested toolchain row (Flutter/AGP/Gradle/Kotlin/JDK/NDK/minSdk) — one tested contract, checked in CI against pinned versions.
5. X10 device matrix CI: arm64 device + x86_64 emulator jobs; RELEASE-mode job (R8/app-bundle) so debug never masks release breakage (codex #9).
6. X9 TTHW enforcement (locked, DX D12): the fresh-machine CI job (create app -> add package -> build -> run) gets timestamps and FAILS if clone->running exceeds the 5-minute budget. TTHW regression = red CI forever. Plus quarterly /devex-review boomerang on the live package (calendar habit — CI can't feel device auth, camera prompts, cold caches).
7. Encryption criterion test (codex #13-eng): capture transport bytes between two peers; assert an authenticated Noise session + decryption only by the intended peer ("not plaintext" alone can pass on framing/encoding).
8. GitHub Discussions + issue templates ON at publish day (TODOS.md item; templates per project_plan.md section 6 — include flutter doctor + worklet log).

ACCEPTANCE: tag -> CI green across matrix (debug+release, device+emulator, fresh-machine under 5 min, licenses enforced, compat table verified, snippets compile) -> packages land on pub.dev in order -> flutter pub add flutter_pear on a clean machine builds and runs the chat demo. pub.dev score >= 130.

title: E5: Data-structure wrappers — Corestore/Hypercore → Hyperbee → Hyperdrive → pairing → Autobase-last
description:
GOAL: Fan out the typed Dart wrappers for the full Pear stack against the locked schema, in DEPENDENCY ORDER (locked — the "any order" premise was wrong): Corestore + Hypercore are the substrate everything sits on and come FIRST; then Hyperbee; then Hyperdrive (streaming risk); then blind-pairing; AUTOBASE LAST (multi-writer risk).

METHOD (locked): hand-write the first two wrappers (PearStore/PearCore against Corestore/Hypercore, then PearBee) directly against the schema, green on the flutter_pear_test fake the day they're written. Decide codegen ONLY if the third wrapper's boilerplate is provably mechanical and repetitive. Every wrapper: expose the JS module over RPC in pear-end, add the typed Dart wrapper — never reimplement any protocol in Dart.

PER-WRAPPER CHECKLIST (applies to every package below):
- pear-end: expose the module over the schema (request/response + events, typed err.codes registered in the error catalog)
- Dart: typed wrapper — Futures for calls, broadcast Streams for events, Uint8List for binary, PearKey for keys, dispose() per Flutter norms, dartdoc on every public symbol
- fake: extend flutter_pear_test in lockstep
- tests: unit tests against the fake (happy + error paths) AND at least one real-device integration test against the real worklet (guards fake/real drift — codex #9)
- licensing: before adding ANY pear-end dependency confirm MIT/Apache-2.0/BSD/ISC; reject GPL/AGPL/LGPL/MPL/SSPL/unlicensed; regenerate THIRD_PARTY_LICENSES via dart run flutter_pear:pack

ORDER + RISK NOTES:
1. PearStore/PearCore (Corestore/Hypercore): substrate. Storage paths per platform. err.codes -> PearStorageException.
2. PearBee (Hyperbee): get/put/del, range streams, watch. Range streams = first real use of event-stream subscribe/unsubscribe.
3. PearDrive (Hyperdrive): BULK RISK. Locked transport decision: file-path import/export (worklet writes to disk, Dart reads the file) or pull-based bounded reads — NOT in-channel streaming unless the throughput benchmark demands it. A bulk transfer large enough to require chunking must complete without exhausting memory.
4. PearPairing (blind-pairing): invites, device linking. Feeds the example app's QR flow.
5. PearBase (Autobase) LAST — HARD. Locked decision (codex #2): do NOT expose generic open/apply (incompatible with zero-JS; Dart callbacks across RPC during deterministic indexing are slow/reentrant/failure-prone). Ship PREBUILT MERGE RECIPES in the pear-end (last-writer-wins, ordered-log, CRDT-map); Dart selects by name. Generic apply = documented custom-worklet advanced path only.

PINNED OPEN QUESTIONS (resolve during, not before): reconnect/delivery semantics after network switch (M1); topic/key persistence, backup & device migration (M2).

Reference: ~/.gstack/projects/andrewloable-flutter_pear/andrewloable-main-design-20260703-131146.md (P2 caveat, Next Steps 3b/4, T12) and project_plan.md section 4 (API coverage map)
acceptance_criteria: Each wrapper: typed Dart API green on the fake day-one + >=1 real-device integration test; Hyperdrive bulk via file-path import/export completes a chunk-scale transfer without exhausting memory; Autobase ships prebuilt recipes (LWW/ordered-log/CRDT-map) selectable by name, NO generic apply in the default path; all pear-end deps license-checked.
notes: CODEX AUDIT FIX: epic-level dependency on E4 removed — the plan's two-track design says Dart-wrapper work proceeds ON THE FAKE in parallel with the native track and 'does not wait on the worklet'. Consequence for each wrapper task: development + fake tests may complete early, but the task CANNOT CLOSE until its real-device integration test runs, which requires flutter_pear-47k.4 (full RPC bridge). Leave the task in_progress with a comment if blocked only on the device test.

title: E6: Lifecycle + hot-restart correctness
description:
GOAL: Lifecycle and hot-restart correctness — constraints with their own success criteria (locked: "schedule them, don't assume they fall out for free"). This is the first thing every dev hits; it ships in v0.1.

WORK ITEMS:
1. Auto suspend/resume wired to AppLifecycleState with sane linger, overridable (opt-out + manual suspend()/resume() stay public on BareWorklet — packages/flutter_pear_bare/lib/src/bare_worklet.dart already has the state machine; this epic wires the Flutter lifecycle observer + linger timer and pipes real suspend/resume through the native side, which currently no-ops in FlutterPearBarePlugin.kt onMethodCall "suspend"/"resume")
2. Hot restart determinism (locked rule): on attach, compare session nonce + bundle version (contract-spine handshake): reattach when the existing worklet is healthy AND version-matched, otherwise cleanly kill and restart. Never require an app reinstall. The native `started` boolean alone loses authority when the engine detaches (codex #6) — worklet identity must live with the worklet, not the plugin instance.
3. Background reality documentation (codex #8 fold): document loudly what Android actually allows (Doze, OEM task killers, foreground-service option) so an OS-killed swarm isn't blamed on the library. Foreground is the supported guarantee; background is best-effort per OS. Surface suspension state via the X8 connection-state stream so end-user apps can render it.

ACCEPTANCE (from plan success criteria):
- Backgrounding suspends the swarm; foregrounding resumes it; linger window honored; override works
- Hot restart with unchanged bundle -> reattach, session continues, no duplicate worklet
- Hot restart with CHANGED pear-end bundle -> kill + clean restart, no stale-worklet conversation
- Doze/OEM-kill behavior documented per-OS with the foreground-service escape hatch

Reference: ~/.gstack/projects/andrewloable-flutter_pear/andrewloable-main-design-20260703-131146.md (Constraints, Next Steps step 5, Success Criteria) and project_plan.md section 6
acceptance_criteria: Background suspends / foreground resumes with linger + override; hot restart: version-matched healthy worklet reattaches, changed bundle kills+restarts cleanly, never reinstall; Doze/OEM-kill reality documented with foreground-service escape hatch; suspension visible on the state stream.

title: E3: flutter_pear_test — in-memory fake swarm/core (schema conformance consumer)
description:
GOAL: Build the flutter_pear_test package — an in-memory fake swarm/core that satisfies the RPC schema so (a) all Dart-wrapper work proceeds in parallel with the risky native track, and (b) app devs can unit-test P2P logic without radios or peers. This is a shipped, first-class package (already in the planned monorepo layout), not throwaway test scaffolding.

AUTHORITY RULE (locked): the fake is a CONFORMANCE CONSUMER of the schema. It never defines the contract. If fake and schema disagree, the schema wins and the fake is fixed.

HONESTY RULE (locked, codex #9): the fake is NOT a conformance test of the real system. Matching RPC envelopes says nothing about Hypercore storage semantics, replication, Autobase ordering, swarm races, or crash behavior. Every data-structure package must ALSO ship at least one real-device integration test against the real worklet (those tests live in each wrapper's epic; this epic provides the fake only).

WORK ITEMS:
1. New package packages/flutter_pear_test with its own pubspec, LICENSE (MIT), CHANGELOG, README (pub.dev score rules apply here too — doc every public symbol)
2. In-memory fake implementing the schema surface used by PearSwarm/PearConnection first: join/leave, connection events, data frames, close; deterministic and instant (no real timers where avoidable)
3. Fake failure injection: expose test hooks to simulate worklet crash, call timeout, stale-nonce frame, connection drop, UDP-blocked network (drives the X8 state machine to 'failed') — these exist so the 7 spine failure-path tests and app-dev tests can exercise error handling
4. Grow the fake surface in lockstep as each wrapper epic lands (fake Corestore/Hypercore/Hyperbee/Hyperdrive/pairing/Autobase recipes)
5. Wire into melos: bootstrap, analyze, test all pass from repo root

ACCEPTANCE: an app dev can write a unit test exercising two fake peers exchanging messages, plus every error path, with zero platform channels, zero radios, zero real peers. flutter_pear's own spine tests run against this fake in CI on every push.

Reference: ~/.gstack/projects/andrewloable-flutter_pear/andrewloable-main-design-20260703-131146.md (Recommended Approach fold-in from C, Next Steps step 2)
acceptance_criteria: App dev can unit-test two fake peers exchanging messages plus every error path with zero radios/peers; failure-injection hooks drive all spine error tests; fake never contradicts schema (schema wins on disagreement).

title: E8.8: Reconcile project_plan.md (+CLAUDE.md status) with the approved design decisions
description:
CONTEXT: E8 docs. Gap found in plan-vs-beads audit (2026-07-03): the design doc's codex #14 finding requires "Reconcile the design doc WITH project_plan.md — do not let them drift", and CLAUDE.md declares project_plan.md the authoritative spec that must be kept in sync when scope changes. Scope HAS changed (office-hours + eng/DX reviews, all locked), but project_plan.md still states the pre-review plan. Today it actively misleads anyone (human or agent) told to treat it as authoritative.

DRIFT TO FIX (project_plan.md vs locked decisions in ~/.gstack/projects/andrewloable-flutter_pear/andrewloable-main-design-20260703-131146.md):
1. Section 5 M1 says "iOS + Android parity" and "CI building both platforms" — LOCKED: v0.1 is Android-only; iOS is v0.2 with its own milestone. Section 6 fresh-machine CI "builds both platforms" likewise.
2. Sections 4+5 stage releases (Corestore 0.2, Hyperbee/Hyperdrive 0.3, Autobase/pairing 0.4) — LOCKED: single v0.1 release with the full stack, no preview slices. Update the coverage-map version column + milestones to the epic structure (E1-E9).
3. Section 6 "Time-to-first-success < 15 minutes" and section 8 "<15 min" — LOCKED (DX D3/D12): champion tier, clone -> two-peer chat < 5 minutes, CI-timed + quarterly human audit.
4. Section 6 "starter repo" — moved post-v0.1, build only on observed friction (bead flutter_pear-uqs).
5. Section 4 PearCrypto topics — reflect the unsafeTopicFromString rename (E8.5) and the invites-first steer.
6. Section 7 "FFI fast path if needed" — reflect the locked bulk decision: benchmark first (E5.1), file-path import/export default, FFI stays escape hatch.
7. Autobase row — reflect prebuilt merge recipes (LWW/ordered-log/CRDT-map), generic apply = custom-worklet advanced path only.
8. Add what the plan never had: connection-state honesty contract (X8/D13), error catalog (X4), doctor tool (X5), desktop CLI peer (X7), session nonce/version handshake, native crash observation.
9. DECIDE-or-DROP while editing (log decisions): section 6 "semantic-release" (superseded by publish-on-tag in E9.7?); section 7 "track upstream releases in CI" (renovate/dependabot for Bare Kit + pear-end pins, or fold into quarterly boomerang?); section 5 M0 "Post intent on holepunchto/bare-kit discussions" (never done — do it or drop it).

ALSO: update the stale CLAUDE.md "Current status" section if it drifts from reality when this lands, and keep the two docs telling one story.

VALIDATION: a cold reader of project_plan.md alone reaches the same plan as the design doc's locked decisions; no remaining contradiction on platforms, release strategy, TTHW, bulk transport, Autobase, or naming; decide-or-drop items each have a logged decision.
acceptance_criteria: project_plan.md agrees with every locked decision (Android-first v0.1 single release, <5min TTHW, file-path bulk, Autobase recipes, unsafeTopicFromString, X8 state contract); coverage map/milestones match the E1-E9 structure; semantic-release / upstream-tracking / bare-kit-discussions items each get a logged decide-or-drop; no doc claims iOS or staged releases.

title: E6.5: Resolve + test reconnect/delivery semantics after network switch (pinned M1 question)
description:
CONTEXT: E6. PINNED OPEN QUESTION from the plan (codex #7-dx fold): reconnect and message-delivery semantics after network switching, brief backgrounding, peer sleep, or process death are UNDEFINED. This is a RESOLVE-the-question task: the deliverable is a decided, documented, tested contract — not a maximal implementation.
FILES: packages/flutter_pear/lib/src/swarm.dart (+pear-end reconnect wiring), docs (contract section), tests.
STEPS:
1. Decide the contract (log via gstack-decision-log + bd comment): what Hyperswarm already gives (it re-announces and re-connects) vs what we add. Recommended lazy-correct posture: connection objects are EPHEMERAL (a drop closes the PearConnection; a new one arrives on the connections stream after reconnect); NO message-delivery guarantee at swarm layer (delivery/ordering is Hypercore replication's job — say so explicitly); state stream reflects reconnecting->connected.
2. Implement only what the contract needs (likely: pear-end rejoin behavior verification + state events already from E2.7).
3. Test: wifi->mobile-data switch and airplane-mode blip on device — old connection closes, new connection arrives, state transitions correct; document expected timings.
4. Write the contract into docs (linked from E6.4 background doc + PearConnection dartdoc).
VALIDATION: device network-switch test matches the documented contract exactly; decision logged; docs cross-linked.

title: E9.6: Noise confidentiality proof — capture-based, with negative control
description:
CONTEXT: E9 (codex #13-eng). The encryption success criterion must be REAL: "payload is not plaintext" can pass on framing/encoding alone. Prove an authenticated Noise session with confidentiality.
FILES: integration test (device or desktop-peer based, packages/flutter_pear_example or a test pkg), possibly a packet-capture harness (tcpdump on the desktop peer side is simplest — capture on the LAPTOP end of a phone<->laptop session from E7.3).
STEPS:
1. Establish a phone<->desktop-peer session; capture transport bytes on the desktop side while sending a known high-entropy-marker plaintext.
2. Assert: the marker does NOT appear in captured bytes; session stats from the worklet confirm a completed Noise handshake with the expected remote key (authenticated, not just encrypted).
3. Negative control: same capture on a raw unencrypted local socket DOES find the marker (proves the harness can see plaintext).
VALIDATION: test runs documented + repeatable (may be semi-manual with the runbook pattern); both assertions + the negative control pass; results posted on the epic.

BEFORE CLOSING — VALIDATION + REVIEW (mandatory, in order):
1. Run: melos run analyze — zero new warnings.
2. Run the task-specific tests named in VALIDATION above — all green. New logic MUST have its test in the same diff (no follow-up-test promises).
3. Self-review the diff line-by-line against ACCEPTANCE below; every criterion demonstrably met, none "mostly".
4. Check the parent epic description: confirm no LOCKED decision was reversed or watered down.
5. Every new/changed public symbol has dartdoc.
6. Non-trivial diff (>50 lines or touches rpc/native/pear-end): run /code-review (or a second-model review) and fix P1 findings before close.
7. Post a closing comment: what changed, test evidence (command + result), anything discovered worth a new bead.
acceptance_criteria: Captured transport bytes never contain the plaintext marker while a raw-socket control does; worklet confirms authenticated handshake with expected key; a wrong-keypair peer fails the handshake and cannot decrypt captures; repeatable runbook + results on the epic.
notes: CODEX AUDIT FIX: add the third assertion — wrong-peer rejection: a third peer with a different keypair attempting to join/decrypt the session is REJECTED (cannot complete the handshake) and captures from the legitimate session are not decryptable with the third peer's keys. 'Intended peer only' must be tested, not inferred from ciphertext opacity.

2. Internet reachability; UDP viability; DHT bootstrap reachability (via worklet op).
3. NAT type estimate (best-effort via hyperswarm/dht info) — name the blocker on hostile networks (maps to E_UDP_BLOCKED guidance).
4. Two-peer loopback self-test where possible (spawn a second in-process peer via the fake or local worklet pair) to separate machine problems from network problems.
VALIDATION: on a normal network: all PASS. On a UDP-blocked network (or firewall sim): doctor NAMES the blocker with the docs anchor — never shrugs. Runs to completion < 30s with clear output on both.

BEFORE CLOSING — VALIDATION + REVIEW (mandatory, in order):
1. Run: melos run analyze — zero new warnings.
2. Run the task-specific tests named in VALIDATION above — all green. New logic MUST have its test in the same diff (no follow-up-test promises).
3. Self-review the diff line-by-line against ACCEPTANCE below; every criterion demonstrably met, none "mostly".
4. Check the parent epic description: confirm no LOCKED decision was reversed or watered down.
5. Every new/changed public symbol has dartdoc.
6. Non-trivial diff (>50 lines or touches rpc/native/pear-end): run /code-review (or a second-model review) and fix P1 findings before close.
7. Post a closing comment: what changed, test evidence (command + result), anything discovered worth a new bead.
acceptance_criteria: dart run flutter_pear:doctor gives PASS/FAIL + one actionable line per check in <30s; on a blocked network it names the blocker with a docs anchor; runtime-only scope (install issues fenced to E8 docs).

description:

title: Recurring: quarterly /devex-review boomerang on the live package (first: ~3mo post-publish)
description:
CONTEXT: DX review decision D12 (locked): TTHW measurement = CI timer (E9.3, catches build-time regressions) + QUARTERLY human /devex-review boomerang on the LIVE package — the human audit catches what CI cannot feel: device authorization prompts, camera permissions, cold Gradle caches on real machines, docs confusion, pairing UX.
CADENCE: first run ~3 months after v0.1 publish; repeat quarterly. Each run: install from pub.dev on a machine that has never built the project, stopwatch the clone/add->two-peer-chat flow against the <5 min champion commitment, run /devex-review, file beads for regressions.
NOTE: this bead is the recurring anchor — after each run, post results as a comment and re-open/defer for the next quarter rather than closing permanently (or clone a fresh bead per run).
acceptance_criteria: Each quarterly run posts: stopwatch TTHW from a cold machine vs the <5 min commitment, /devex-review score, and beads filed for any regression; bead re-opened/deferred for the next cycle.

title: Post-v0.1: starter template repo (build only on observed friction)
description:
CONTEXT: Post-v0.1 DevEx item from project_plan.md section 6, deliberately excluded from the v0.1 epics: a `flutter create --template`-style starter repo. For v0.1 the committed example app + README quickstart cover time-to-first-success; this exists to reduce day-one friction for NEW apps (not clones) if adoption data shows people struggle to wire flutter_pear into a fresh project.
TRIGGER: revisit when post-publish issues/questions show new-project setup friction (watch GitHub issues + Discussions after v0.1). If no friction signal appears, close as not-needed rather than building speculatively.
SCOPE when triggered: a template repo (or `dart create` brick) with flutter_pear pre-wired: dependency, lifecycle setup, a minimal joined-swarm screen, doctor invocation in README.
acceptance_criteria: Either a published starter template that a new user can create-and-run in under 5 minutes, OR a documented close-as-not-needed decision citing absence of setup-friction signals.
